findings.reachability: Authenticated users with install_plugins capability (or lower if cei_allow_unsafe_imports filter is hooked) who can reach the WordPress Customizer import flow
findings.justification: The import handler calls `@unserialize(trim($raw), array('allowed_classes' => false))` on the contents of an attacker-controlled uploaded file; while `allowed_classes => false` mitigates object injection on PHP 7+, servers running PHP < 7 ignore this option entirely and are fully vulnerable to PHP object injection leading to RCE via magic method chains, and even on PHP 7+ the file content is fully attacker-controlled after only an extension check.

detection_pattern.section_name: PHP Object Injection: unserialize on user-supplied data
detection_pattern.description: Matches calls to unserialize() which, when fed attacker-controlled data, can trigger PHP object injection via __wakeup/__destruct gadget chains; files lacking json_decode or allowed_classes=>false are especially suspect.

findings.reachability: Authenticated users with install_plugins capability who upload a crafted .dat import file with image URLs pointing to internal/cloud-metadata endpoints and check the 'Download and import image files?' checkbox
findings.justification: When the import-images checkbox is set, `_import_images()` iterates over mod values from the attacker-controlled deserialized data and calls `download_url($val)` on any string matching an image extension pattern, allowing the server to make arbitrary HTTP GET requests to attacker-specified URLs including internal network addresses such as cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254).

detection_pattern.section_name: SSRF: download_url called on user-supplied URL
detection_pattern.description: Matches download_url() calls that may receive attacker-controlled URLs from deserialized or uploaded data; without URL allow-listing or scheme/host validation, this enables SSRF against internal network resources.

findings.reachability: Authenticated users with edit_theme_options capability who can trigger an import error; the XSS executes in the Customizer admin page for any subsequent visitor with edit_theme_options
findings.justification: The global `$cei_error` is printed directly into a `<script>alert("...");</script>` block without any escaping via `esc_js()` or `wp_json_encode()`; if any error path sets $cei_error to a value containing `");` the script context is broken and arbitrary JavaScript executes in the admin context.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: unescaped variable in inline script alert
detection_pattern.description: Matches dynamic values echoed directly into JavaScript alert() calls inside script tags; without esc_js() the injected string can escape the alert and execute arbitrary JS.

findings.reachability: Any code or plugin that hooks the 'cei_allow_unsafe_imports' filter can grant import capability to arbitrary or unauthenticated users
findings.justification: `can_import()` returns `apply_filters('cei_allow_unsafe_imports', current_user_can('install_plugins'))`, meaning any plugin or theme that returns `true` from this filter unconditionally bypasses the install_plugins capability requirement and allows any user to perform the import, which itself can trigger unserialize and SSRF.

detection_pattern.description: Matches capability checks wrapped in apply_filters where the filter result replaces (rather than gates) the current_user_can() result; this pattern lets third-party code silently downgrade the required capability.

findings.reachability: Authenticated users with install_plugins capability performing a customizer import
findings.justification: `wp_check_filetype()` validates only the file extension (must be `.dat`), not actual file content or MIME type; the `wp_handle_upload` override explicitly sets `test_type => false` disabling WordPress's real MIME sniffing, so any file with a `.dat` extension (including PHP scripts) is accepted and processed by unserialize.

detection_pattern.section_name: File Upload: MIME type validation disabled
detection_pattern.description: Matches wp_handle_upload override arrays that set test_type=>false, disabling WordPress's real MIME type sniffing and relying solely on file extension checks that are trivially bypassed.
